region_of_origin: United States (Nashville, Tennessee); served in Singapore context

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific 'hot chicken' or 'spicy fried chicken' entry; closest defensible match is the general fried chicken food product class (FOODON_00002725).
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for a spiced fried chicken dish; no dedicated hot chicken code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for hot chicken or Nashville-style spicy fried chicken. A039V (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for hot chicken or spicy fried chicken dish as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of hot chicken is approximately 350 g as a bowl, or 450 g as a bento rice bowl set; the 350 g bowl is used as the reference serving for glycemic load calculation.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and Spicy Chicken — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for hot/spicy fried chicken at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (0.57 g/100 g), trans fat (0.0 g/100 g), and vitamin C (2.29 mg/100 g) were carried from the input record attributed to a delivery platform source; these three values are treated as estimated and flagged accordingly. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and remaining micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nashville-style hot chicken. GI estimated at 45 based on the analogous food 'fried chicken' from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, which report GI values for battered/breaded fried chicken in the range 40–50 (low GI). The low carbohydrate content of this dish (6.18 g/100 g) means the glycemic load per 350 g serving is low: GL = (45 × 6.18 × 3.5) / 100 = 9.73 g; however, the dish as served in a bento rice bowl set (450 g) would substantially increase GL due to the rice component. GL reported here is for the 350 g bowl serving without rice: GL = (45 × 6.18 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 9.7, rounded to 9.7. Corrected calculation: GL = GI × carb_g_per_serving / 100 = 45 × (6.18 × 3.5) / 100 = 45 × 21.63 / 100 = 9.73. Reported as 9.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 842 mg/100 g — substantially exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold; a 350 g bowl delivers approximately 2,947 mg sodium, nearly 130% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request sauces and condiments on the side, avoid adding soy sauce or chilli sauce, and compensate with very low-sodium choices for the remainder of the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation contributes high fat (20.2 g/100 g) that impairs insulin sensitivity; when served as a bento rice bowl the glycemic load rises substantially due to the white rice component.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should choose the bowl serving without rice, request reduced sauce to limit added sugars, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the overall glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (842 mg/100 g) combined with deep-fried fat (20.2 g/100 g total fat) and cholesterol (102 mg/100 g) elevates cardiovascular risk; the saturated fat value of 0.57 g/100 g appears unusually low for a deep-fried product and should be interpreted with caution.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, choose grilled chicken alternatives where available, and avoid the high-sodium sauces that are the primary driver of cardiovascular risk in this preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (842 mg/100 g) worsens inflammatory tissue swelling; chilli-based spice blend contains nightshade compounds that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to the combination of high sodium, nightshade-derived chilli, and AGE-generating deep-frying; if consumed, request reduced chilli and sauce and choose a smaller portion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot Chicken is a Western-origin spicy fried chicken dish popularised in Nashville, Tennessee, USA, and widely served in Singapore's casual dining and fast-food outlets. It consists of a chicken breast or thigh that is coated in a seasoned flour batter, deep-fried until crispy, then dusted or coated with a hot pepper spice blend that delivers intense heat and a characteristic reddish-orange colour. The dish is typically served topped with creamy coleslaw and sliced pickles, which provide cooling contrast to the spice. In Singapore, it is commonly offered as a standalone bowl (approximately 350 g) or as a bento rice bowl set (approximately 450 g) with steamed white rice. Nutritionally, hot chicken is energy-dense at 272 kcal/100 g, with a high fat content of 20.2 g/100 g from deep-frying and a moderate protein content of 16.78 g/100 g from the chicken. The sodium content is very high at 842 mg/100 g, making it clinically significant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The carbohydrate content is relatively low at 6.18 g/100 g, giving the dish a low glycemic index when consumed without rice. The dish is generally considered halal in Singapore when prepared with halal-certified chicken and ingredients. Clinical caution is warranted for hypertensive, cardiovascular, and rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the high sodium load and pro-inflammatory preparation method.
common_user_queries: How many calories in hot chicken?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo at 0.95 confidence. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values were originally attributed to a delivery platform (GrabFood), which is a forbidden primary source; these three values are treated as estimated and flagged accordingly. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and most micronutrients are not available in the HPB record and are set to -1. GI is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profiles and extended micronutrient data for this hot chicken preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 2. Saturated fat (0.57 g/100 g) is unusually low for a deep-fried chicken product; typical deep-fried chicken saturated fat values range from 2–5 g/100 g. This value was originally attributed to GrabFood (a forbidden primary source) and should be re-verified against an approved source. It is retained here as an estimate pending re-verification. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g (confirmed zero per input record attributed to GrabFood; treat as estimated). 4. Vitamin C (2.29 mg/100 g) is plausible given the presence of coleslaw (cabbage) and pickles; value retained as estimated from input. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on fried chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no direct measurement for hot chicken exists. 6. The input record contained two portions both flagged is_default=true; the 350 g bowl is used as the primary reference serving for GL calculation. 7. The saturated_fat_g value of 0.57 g/100 g should be flagged for re-verification in a future review cycle.
